When Money Gets Expensive  ·  Economics, Unit 1
Page 1 of 15
Start here · No background needed

When money gets expensive

How prices, interest rates, and one institution in Washington shape your everyday life.

Almost nobody buys a car, a phone, or a house by paying all at once. Most people borrow the money — and borrowing has a price. Prices for everything else rise and fall too. And behind a lot of it sits one institution most people never learn about.

This unit builds the whole picture from the ground up. We start with the most basic force in any economy — why prices move at all — then work up to interest rates, the Federal Reserve, and inflation, the story in the news right now.

We'll define every new word the moment it appears. And by the end, you won't just read about how this works. You'll sit in the chair and make the call yourself.

🎛️  Coming up: you become the decision-maker.
Part 1 · The most basic force

Let's start with one word: demand

Picture the newest phone everyone wants. The morning it drops, people line up before sunrise. They want it, and they're ready to pay for it. That eagerness to buy has a name: demand.

Demand is simply how much of something people want and are able to buy. When a lot of people want something, we say demand is high. When barely anyone does, demand is low.

Demand rises and falls all the time. A heat wave sends demand for air conditioners soaring. The week after the holidays, demand for gift wrap falls off a cliff. Same product, totally different demand.

📌 LOCKED IN

Demand — how much of something people want and are able to buy. More eager buyers means higher demand.

Part 1 · The most basic force

Now its partner: supply

For every buyer, there's someone on the other side making and selling the thing. How much of it they put out for sale is called supply.

Supply is how much of a product is available to buy. A bakery that makes 200 loaves of bread has a supply of 200. If a frost wipes out half the orange crop, the supply of oranges drops.

Here's the part that matters. Demand and supply are almost never perfectly matched — one side is usually bigger than the other. And when they're out of balance, something completely predictable happens to price. That's the next page, and it's the key to this whole unit.

📌 LOCKED IN

Supply — how much of a product is available to buy. More goods on offer means higher supply.

Part 1 · The most basic force

The seesaw: how prices actually move

Think of demand and supply as two ends of a seesaw, with price balanced in the middle. Tip one side, and the price has to move. The rule, in plain terms:

When lots of buyers chase too few goods, sellers can charge more, so prices rise. When there's plenty to go around but few buyers, sellers cut prices to move it, so prices fall. That's the engine behind nearly every price you've ever paid.

Try it. Set how many buyers there are and how much is available, and watch what happens to price.

🧍 How many buyers want it?
📦 How much is available to sell?
→
Prices hold fairly steady
Plenty of buyers, but enough goods to meet them. The seesaw is roughly level.

Hold onto the "lots of buyers, only a little available" setting — prices shoot up. That exact situation, happening across the whole economy at once, has a name you've heard on the news. We'll get there soon.

Quick check ✓

Quick gut-check

True or False: When lots of buyers want something but there's very little of it available, prices tend to go down.
✅ Correct. When demand is high but supply is low, buyers are competing for a scarce thing — so sellers raise prices, not lower them. Picture tickets to a sold-out show: tiny supply, huge demand, and the price goes through the roof. You've got the seesaw down.
🔄 Flip it — prices go UP here, not down. Easy to mix up, so no worries. But picture the seesaw: lots of buyers + barely any goods means people are competing for something scarce, and sellers can charge more. Think of a sold-out concert — tiny supply, massive demand, ticket prices skyrocket. High demand + low supply = prices up.
Part 2 · The price of borrowing

Now — what is "interest"?

We've covered the price of things. Now meet the price of borrowing money.

Imagine you borrow $100 from a friend. A year later, they ask for $110 back. That extra $10? That is interest.

Interest is the cost of borrowing money. The easiest way to picture it: it's like rent. When you borrow money, you're renting it for a while — and the rent you pay for using it is called interest. The bigger the rent, the more borrowing costs you. And that "rent" has a name you'll hear constantly: the interest rate.

📌 TWO WORDS, LOCKED IN

Interest — the extra money you pay on top of what you borrowed.

Interest rate — how big that extra is, shown as a percent (like 5%).

Part 2 · The price of borrowing

Why a tiny number is a big deal

Here's the trap. An interest rate looks small — 3%? 6%? Who cares? But spread across a big loan over many years, those few points quietly turn into thousands of dollars.

Try it yourself. Below is a $25,000 car loan paid back over 5 years. Drag the rate up and down. Watch the monthly payment — and the total you hand over by the end.

Interest rate6.0%
2% · cheap12% · pricey
Monthly payment
$483
Total paid back
$29,000
Extra "rent" (interest)
$4,000
A middle-of-the-road rate — the extra is starting to add up.

Same car. Same five years. The only thing that changed was the rate — and it quietly changed what the car cost you. Now picture that same effect on a house, borrowed over 30 years. That's why this one number touches nearly every big decision in your life.

Quick check ✓

Let's make sure that landed

True or False: A higher interest rate means borrowing money costs you less.
✅ Correct — and here's exactly why. A higher rate means a bigger "rent" on the money you borrow. On that $25,000 car loan you just played with, sliding from 4% up to 10% didn't change the car at all — but it could add thousands to what you pay by the end. Higher rate = more expensive borrowing. You've got the core idea this whole unit is built on.
🔄 Not quite — flip it around. This one's easy to trip on, because "lower number = less money" just feels right. But remember the rent metaphor: a higher rate is a higher rent on borrowed money. Picture the slider you just moved — when you dragged the rate up, the total you paid went up too, not down. So higher rate = more expensive, not less. (Totally normal to miss this the first time.)
Part 3 · The decision-maker

So who actually sets the rate?

Here's the part most people never learn. One institution in the United States sets a key interest rate — and nearly every other rate (car loans, mortgages, credit cards) follows its lead.

It's called the Federal Reserve. Everyone just calls it "the Fed."

Picture it as the thermostat for the cost of borrowing. Turn the dial up, and borrowing gets more expensive across the whole country. Turn it down, and borrowing gets cheaper. That single dial is the most powerful lever in the economy.

📌 LOCKED IN

The Federal Reserve ("the Fed") — the central bank of the United States. One of its main jobs is setting a key interest rate that shapes the cost of borrowing everywhere.

📊 Right now (2026): the Fed has the dial set at about 3.5% – 3.75%. This is the exact number argued about on the news almost every week.
Part 3 · The decision-maker

Why the Fed is kept out of politics

Here's something surprising: the people who run the Fed are not elected, and by design, politicians cannot order them to move the dial. Why keep such a powerful tool out of the government's direct control?

Because politicians and the Fed often want different things. A politician usually prefers low rates — cheap borrowing boosts spending and jobs, which is popular before an election. But cheap money for too long can let inflation run wild. The Fed is built to take the long view: protect the value of your money, even when the right move is unpopular. That separation has a name: the Fed's independence.

📌 LOCKED IN

Fed independence — the idea that the Fed should set interest rates based on what the economy needs, not on what's politically popular at the moment.

And this isn't just theory right now. In 2026 the Fed got a new leader, Chair Kevin Warsh, after a tense national fight over exactly this question: should the Fed bow to political pressure to cut rates, or stay independent? The clip below is the previous chair, Jerome Powell, defending that independence on his way out. Listen for what he says is really at stake.

▶
▶ PBS NewsHour · video

Powell defends the Fed's independence as his term ends

Watch on PBS  ↗  (opens in a new tab)
Part 4 · The thing in the news

Now inflation makes sense

Remember the seesaw? Hold that picture, because inflation is just that seesaw, tipped — across the entire economy at once.

Here's the connection. When borrowing is cheap and everyone has money to spend, demand surges everywhere. But factories and farms can only make so much, so supply can't keep up. Lots of buyers, not enough goods — and exactly like the seesaw told you, prices rise across the board. When prices keep climbing like this, it's called inflation, and it quietly shrinks your money: the same $20 buys less than it did last year.

📌 LOCKED IN

Inflation — when prices across the economy rise over time, so each dollar you have buys a little less.

Prices can also rise from the other side of the seesaw — when supply suddenly drops. That's exactly what's happening right now.

📊 Right now (2026): U.S. inflation is about 3.8% — the fastest in years. A big reason: conflict in the Middle East squeezed the world's oil supply, pushing gas above $4 a gallon. Less supply → higher prices, which then ripple into groceries, airfare, and almost everything that has to be shipped.

The clip below shows how that gas-price spike reaches ordinary shoppers — the seesaw playing out in real life, on real receipts.

▶
▶ CNBC · video

How the oil-price spike is the first hit of inflation

Watch on CNBC  ↗  (opens in a new tab)

So here's the Fed's hard choice. When inflation runs too hot, the Fed turns the dial up — making borrowing expensive on purpose — so people spend less and prices stop climbing so fast. It cools inflation by gently cooling the whole economy. Which sets up the exact question you're about to answer.

Quick check ✓

Last check before you take the chair

True or False: The Fed sometimes raises interest rates on purpose — even though it makes borrowing more expensive for ordinary people.
✅ Exactly right. It feels strange, but yes — when inflation runs too hot, the Fed deliberately makes borrowing more expensive. The goal is to nudge people to spend a little less, so prices stop rising so fast (remember the seesaw — fewer eager buyers eases the pressure on prices). It isn't cruelty; it's the Fed using its one big lever to protect the value of your money. You're about to feel exactly why this is so hard.
🔄 Actually — it's true. This one surprises almost everyone, so good instinct to question it. But the Fed really does raise rates on purpose when inflation is too high. Trace it through the seesaw: expensive borrowing → people spend less → fewer buyers chasing goods → prices cool down. Making borrowing pricey is the Fed's main tool against inflation, even though it stings short-term. You'll see the catch in a moment.
Part 5 · You decide

Meet John — before you decide his fate

To feel how this works, meet John.

John owns a car dealership in a busy town — let's say he sells Subarus. His whole business runs on one simple thing: people taking out loans to buy cars.

So when you — as the Fed — move the interest-rate dial, you're not moving an abstract number. You're reaching right into John's dealership. You're deciding whether his lot is packed with buyers… or empty. Whether he hires more people… or cuts their hours.

And John isn't alone. You'll also watch Sarah, who wants to buy a home. Jane, who just wants her groceries to stop getting more expensive. And jobs across the whole town.

Turn the page. The dial is yours.

🎛️ You are the Fed

Your move.

Inflation is running a little hot — prices keep climbing and people are feeling it. You hold the one big lever. Flip between the two real choices the Fed weighs, and watch the ripples spread through town. There's no score. Just notice: each choice helps someone and hurts someone else.
🔽Cut rates LOWmake borrowing cheap
🔼Raise rates HIGHmake borrowing expensive
Pick one of the two choices above. Then try the other. 👆
🚗John's Dealership
— waiting —

Choose a rate to see how John's lot looks.

🏠Sarah · wants a home
— waiting —

Choose a rate to see Sarah's plans.

🛒Jane · groceries
— waiting —

Choose a rate to see what's happening to prices.

💼Jobs in town
— waiting —

Choose a rate to see the job market.

The trade-off you just made Pick a rate and I'll tell you who you just helped — and who you just asked to wait.
Now flip to the other choice. Notice there's no setting where everyone wins. 👀
The big idea

There is no free move.

That feeling you just had — each choice helped someone and hurt someone else — that's not a flaw in the simulation. That's the actual job.

When the real Fed meets, this is the room they're in. Cut rates, and you risk letting prices keep eating people's paychecks. Raise them, and you risk emptying John's lot and costing jobs. They are always trading one pain for another. There is no dial setting that makes everyone happy — only which trade-off you can live with.

So next time you hear that "the Fed raised rates" or "the Fed held rates steady," you'll know what's really happening underneath: someone just chose which group to protect, and which group to ask to wait. And now you know exactly how that decision ripples all the way down — from a headline in Washington to John's quiet car lot, and to the price on Jane's grocery receipt.

✍️ Before you go — sit with this one:

If you were on the Fed right now, with inflation running a little hot — would you raise the rate to protect Jane's grocery budget, even knowing it might cost John's employees their hours? There's no right answer. What matters is that you can now see who each choice touches.

WHAT YOU CAN NOW EXPLAIN TO SOMEONE ELSE:
  • ✔  Supply & demand — the seesaw that sets nearly every price
  • ✔  What an interest rate is — the rent on borrowed money
  • ✔  What the Fed does, and why it's kept independent of politics
  • ✔  What inflation is — the seesaw tipping across the whole economy
  • ✔  The trade-off at the heart of every Fed decision
